error.signature: Date calculations off by 1 day when converting Excel serial numbers, or Feb 29 1900 appears in data

verdict.summary: Excel inherited a deliberate bug from Lotus 1-2-3: it treats 1900 as a leap year (Feb 29, 1900 exists as serial number 60). This makes all serial numbers before March 1, 1900 off by 1 day. Microsoft kept this for backwards compatibility.

dead_ends.action: Convert Excel serial number to date by adding days to Jan 1, 1900
dead_ends.why_fails: Serial number 1 = Jan 1, 1900 in Excel, but the fake Feb 29 1900 means dates after Feb 28 1900 are correct while earlier dates are off by 1.

dead_ends.action: Assume all Excel dates are correct after conversion
dead_ends.why_fails: Any date before March 1, 1900 is off by 1 day. Serial number 60 maps to the non-existent Feb 29, 1900.

workarounds.action: Use xlrd, openpyxl, or pandas to read Excel dates (they handle the 1900 bug)
workarounds.how: import pandas as pd; df = pd.read_excel('file.xlsx')  # automatically handles serial date conversion

workarounds.action: If manually converting: subtract 1 from serial numbers <= 60, account for the epoch offset
workarounds.how: from datetime import datetime, timedelta; date = datetime(1899, 12, 30) + timedelta(days=serial)  # note: Dec 30 not Jan 1

workarounds.action: Validate by checking for Feb 29, 1900 as a sentinel for corrupted data
workarounds.how: if date == datetime(1900, 2, 29): raise ValueError('Excel 1900 leap year bug detected')
